SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claims — that a joint CTC-attention model can regularize attention alignments and that this approach yields competitive results on CSJ and HKUST compared to conventional hybrid HMM/DNN systems — are supported by experiments and comparisons to appropriate baselines (CTC and hybrid systems). The proposed methodology (joint CTC objective attached to an attention encoder and combined decoding/rescoring) is technically coherent and hyperparameters are reported. Minor technical shortcomings include no mention of statistical significance testing and the fact the method did not surpass the strongest TDNN + LF-MMI baseline, but these do not invalidate the central technical claims.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear progression from motivation to method (joint CTC-attention) to experiments on CSJ and HKUST and to conclusions, and the main contributions are stated early. Related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is reported as good, with hyperparameters provided, which aids reproducibility. However, some presentation details are missing in the summary (evaluation metrics not specified, figures/tables effectiveness not mentioned, and code availability unclear), suggesting minor gaps in clarity or completeness. Overall it reads well but has small presentation omissions that prevent an exceptional rating.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a concrete joint CTC–attention framework that both regularizes the attention encoder with a CTC objective and integrates CTC probabilities into decoding (including second-pass rescoring), which is a clear methodological combination beyond purely CTC or purely attention models. This approach is framed to specifically address irregular alignments (repetitions, deletions, insertions) and to obtain competitive performance without linguistic resources on CSJ and HKUST, distinguishing it from the listed baselines (CTC-only, attention-only, and hybrid HMM/DNN systems). Given the described contributions and their clear differentiation from prior work, the novelty is notable though largely a non-trivial combination and extension of existing techniques rather than a radical new paradigm.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed joint CTC-attention model on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against several strong baselines (hybrid HMM/DNN, CTC variants, TDNN lf-MMI), which supports the main claims. However, the summary does not report ablation studies or sensitivity analyses for key design choices (e.g., weighting between CTC and attention, decoding rescoring strategies), and evaluation metrics are not specified, limiting assessment of empirical strength. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and some limitations are acknowledged (did not reach TDNN lf-MMI performance; attention irregularities), but reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters reported but code not mentioned).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a range of appropriate and recent baselines—including hybrid HMM/DNN (Kaldi), CTC-based systems (Kanda et al., Miao et al.) and a strong TDNN with lattice-free sequence-discriminative training (Povey et al., 2016)—and evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST). It also explicitly acknowledges that it did not reach TDNN LF-MMI performance and reports hyperparameters with thorough related-work coverage. However, the summary does not specify evaluation metrics or fully confirm identical evaluation conditions, so fairness of some comparisons is unclear, hence a small deduction.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—stabilizing attention-based end-to-end ASR—by jointly training a CTC objective with an attention encoder and combining scores during decoding, demonstrating comparable performance to hybrid HMM/DNN systems on CSJ and HKUST without linguistic resources. Showing that CTC regularization reduces attention alignment errors (repetitions, deletions, insertions) and can be integrated into decoding is likely to change how researchers design end-to-end ASR systems and opens clear new directions for multi-objective training and decoding strategies. It is not fully transformative because it did not surpass TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training, but it represents a strong, widely applicable contribution to the field.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses end-to-end ASR by jointly training CTC and attention models and combining scores at decoding, a methodological contribution of clear interest to the ML/NLP community. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ, HKUST) and compares to strong hybrid and CTC baselines, using evaluation and reporting practices expected at top venues. The work is somewhat speech-specialized and did not surpass the TDNN + LF-MMI result, so it may be a better fit for a top speech conference, but it still fits well within ML/NLP venues focused on sequence models and end-to-end learning.

main_claims: Proposes a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR model that utilizes benefits of both CTC and attention mechanisms during training and decoding.
Uses a CTC objective attached to an attention-based encoder as a regularization to guide/constraint the attention alignment and reduce irregular alignments (repetitions, deletions, insertions).

Applies the proposed method to Japanese and Mandarin benchmarks and shows comparable performance to conventional hybrid HMM/DNN systems without requiring linguistic resources (e.g., pronunciation dictionaries, tokenization).
Combines CTC probabilities (computed by the forward algorithm) with attention-based decoding, including a second-pass rescoring using CTC-attention probabilities.
_summary.methodology: Attach a CTC objective to an attention-based encoder and jointly train the CTC and attention objectives; during decoding, combine attention and CTC scores (including a second-pass rescoring using CTC probabilities computed by the CTC forward algorithm) to obtain hypotheses.
datasets: HKUST Mandarin Chinese conversational telephone speech recognition (MTS)
baselines: DNN-hybrid (Moriya et al., 2015) / hybrid HMM/DNN (Kaldi recipe)
CTC with language model (Miao et al., 2016)
TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training (Povey et al., 2016)
limitations: The proposed method did not reach the performance obtained by TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training.
Attention-based models can produce irregular alignments (repetitions, deletions, insertions), which the joint method aims to mitigate.
_summary.topic_area: Speech recognition - end-to-end ASR (CTC and attention hybrid)
